architecture.summary: The paper constructs a graph-structured representation of sparse autoencoder (SAE) features by modeling each feature as a token co-occurrence graph, where nodes are frequent tokens near strong activations and edges connect co-occurring token pairs within local context windows. A custom Weisfeiler-Lehman style frequency-binned graph kernel computes similarity over this structural space. Features are then clustered using this graph-based similarity, and results are compared against decoder cosine similarity and token-histogram baselines.

biblio.title: From Token Lists to Graph Motifs: Weisfeiler-Lehman Analysis of Sparse Autoencoder Features

claims.dataset: synthetic mixed-domain corpus probed on GPT-2 Small SAE
claims.statement: Graph-based clustering recovers heuristic motif families (punctuation-heavy patterns, language and script clusters, and code-like templates) that are not recovered by clustering on decoder cosine similarity

claims.dataset: synthetic mixed-domain corpus probed on GPT-2 Small SAE
claims.statement: A token-histogram baseline achieves higher overall purity than graph-based clustering
The contribution of the graph view is complementary rather than dominant: it surfaces structural relationships that token-frequency and decoder-weight views alone do not capture

claims.dataset: synthetic mixed-domain corpus probed on GPT-2 Small SAE
claims.statement: Cluster assignments are stable across graph-construction hyperparameters and random seeds
